english: kyis) dkr. (dkr.), to wrap a garment round the body; rtsá-bar k'ór-lo dkris-pai yceu (rtsa bar 'khor lo dkris pai yi'u) Med. was explained to me: a magic spell in circular writing, wrapped round the lower end of a clyster-pipe, fig.: sér-snas kún-nas dkris (ser snas kun nas dkris) quite ensnared in avarice; kun-dkris (kun dkris) 'all-ensnaring' = sin. — dkrí-ma (dkri ma) (Glr. 47. where the text has dríma (dri ma)) means very likely necker-chief, which col. is called *kog-ṭí* (kog ti) or *ka-ṭí* (ka ti) C. *'og-śri* ('og shri), *ka-śri* (ka shri), *kya-śri* (kya shri) W. — In the sense of k'ríd-pa (khrid pa) to conduct (Sch.) it never came to my notice.

1. darkened, obscured, dim, = k'rígs-pa (khrigs pa). — also dkrigs-p'rág (dkrigs phrag), term for a very large number, Cs.: a 100 000 billion, acc to Zam. = ytáms-pa (gtams pa), which Cs. renders, a 1000 billion. The one may be, after all, as correct as the other, for all thėse large numbers are, of course, not meant to be used in serious calculations, but are mere imitations of fantastic Indian extravagancies.

pf. dkrugs (dkrugs) (W. *śrug-če* (shrug ce)) to stir, stir up, agitate (as the storm does the sea); to trouble, disturb, confound (as enemies of religion confound the doctrine, or as passions disturb the mind); dkrugs-śiṅ (dkrugs shing) 1. stirring-stick, twirling-stick. 2. disturber, enemy e.g. of the doctrine Glr. — dkrugs (dkrugs) Schr.: turning-lathe (?) — dkrugs-ma (dkrugs ma) Schr.: quarrel. — Dzl. 22, 7. dkrugs byéd-pa (dkrugs byed pa) dubious; a safer reading is dkú-lto byéd-pa (dku lto byed pa).
(= skróg-pa (skrog pa)) 1. to stir, churn frq. 2. to rouse, scare up, Glr. — 3. to wag e.g. the tail W.
v. gégs-pa (gegs pa); bkág-ča byéd-pa (bkag cha byed pa) to forbid Sch.

? Lex. quote t'ágs-kyi bkad (thags kyi bkad), which was explained to me by: the crossing of threads in weaving; similar: mgó-spui bkad (mgo spui bkad), mgo-bkád (mgo bkad) the crossing or entangling of the hair on the head. — bkád-pa (bkad pa) seems = k'ád-pa ('khad pa).
1. a bake-house, kitchen, cook's shop Lex. — 2. open hall or shed, erected on festive occasions Tib.-Ssk. Glossary; Tar. 18, 12.

to put, to press, rkáṅ-pa (rkang pa) rtsíg-pa-la (rtsig pa la) one's foot against a wall; to apply, ɣśó-mo (gsho mo) the plane, lág-pa (lag pa) the hand Zam., to put the hand on or to something (or: stretch it out? Sch.)

bka (bka') (resp. for ɣtam (gtam), tsig (tshig), skad (skad)) word, speech of a respected person (wherefore order, commandment may often be substituted for it), rgyál-poi bka (rgyal poi bka') the word of the king, bká-la ɣtógs-pa (bka' la gtogs pa) to belong to the word, i. e. to be under the commandment or dominion (of somebody) Glr.; rgyál-bai bka (rgyal bai bka') the word of Buddha (this is named as one of the five 'means of grace', so to speak, Glr. fol. 70; the four others are: mdo-rgyúd (mdo rgyud) the sacred writings (sutra and tantra), bstán-bcós (bstan bcos) doctrinal and scientific writings (śāstra), luṅ (lung) oral benedictions and instructions of Lamas, man-ṅág (man ngag) admonitions given by them). After quotations bka (bka') or bkao (bka'o) (= skad (skad) & skad-do (skad do)) means: thus says (the holy book or teacher). bka (bka') as first part of a compound is frequently used to give the word adjoined the character of respectfulness, and is therefore not to be translated separately. Phrases and compounds: bka bkód-pa (bka' bkod pa) to publish, proclaim; publication, proclamation C. — bka-bkyón (bka' bkyon) (col. *kab-kyon* (kab kyon)) 'verbal blows' reprimand, rebuke (given by a superior), bka-bkyón byéd-pa (bka' bkyon byed pa), mdzád-pa (mdzad pa) B. C., *tón-če* (ton ce), *píṅ-če* (ping ce) W., bka bkyón-pa (bka' bkyon pa), all of them construed with dat., to rebuke somebody. — bka-k'ríms (bka' khrims) law, commandment, rgyál-poi bka-k'ríms ɣnyán-pas (rgyal poi bka' khrims gnyan pas) by the cruel order of the king Dzl. — bka bgró-ba (bka' bgro ba) to consult, to deliberate, naṅ-blón bė́u (nang blon be'u)
